skill.name: pubchem-database
skill.description: Query PubChem with PUG-REST and PubChemPy for compound search, molecular properties, structure matching, bioactivity data, and identifier conversion.
skill.summary: Search PubChem compounds, properties, structures, identifiers, and bioactivity data from AI coding tools.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: AI review found that most static findings are false positives from Markdown code fences, PubChem API path examples, and chemistry identifiers such as SMILES and InChI. Confirmed risks are legitimate external network access to PubChem and optional file downloads that can overwrite caller-selected paths. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, shell execution, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: External Network Access to PubChem
medium_findings.description: The helper script builds PubChem and PUG-View URLs and performs GET or POST requests. This is expected for the skill, but users should understand that compound names, CIDs, SMILES, targets, and assay identifiers are sent to PubChem.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network calls are directly present and target public PubChem endpoints. The intent appears legitimate, with no evidence of credential or local data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Download Helper Can Overwrite Files
medium_findings.description: The compound download helper passes a caller-provided filename to PubChemPy with overwrite enabled. This is useful for structure export, but unsafe callers could overwrite files if they pass unintended paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The overwrite behavior is explicit in the helper and examples. Risk depends on caller control of filenames, so this is a publishable concern rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer interpreted Markdown code fences and inline backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The cited locations are documentation examples, not executable shell command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown fences, API route examples, or prose. No subprocess, os.system, shell=True, or equivalent command execution was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high blocker alerts resolve to chemistry and API documentation text, including SMILES, InChI, InChIKey, GET routes, and PUG-View. No cryptographic algorithm implementation or security-sensitive hashing was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static hits do not correspond to crypto APIs or digest operations. The surrounding context is chemical identifier documentation and PubChem endpoint text.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Workflow Text
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts point to loop limits, result counting, troubleshooting, and example output for PubChem workflows. They do not inspect host users, processes, filesystems, network interfaces, or environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code handles PubChem result sets and prints example assay information. No host reconnaissance behavior was identified.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Requests to External API
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses requests.get and requests.post against PubChem endpoints. This is necessary for live PubChem data, but it creates an outbound network dependency.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact HTTP calls and public PubChem URLs are present. The calls are domain-appropriate and not hidden.

dangerous_patterns.title: File Write Through Structure Downloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples and helper support writing downloaded structure data to local files. Users should choose output paths deliberately and avoid untrusted filenames.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: File output is explicit and limited to downloaded PubChem content. The main concern is overwrite behavior when filename input is not controlled.

content.value_statement: Chemical research often needs fast access to compound identifiers, properties, structures, and assay records. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through PubChem queries using documented APIs and helper scripts.
actual_capabilities: Search PubChem compounds by chemical name, CID, SMILES, InChI, or molecular formula.
Retrieve molecular properties such as formula, weight, XLogP, TPSA, and hydrogen bond counts.
Run similarity and substructure searches with PubChemPy and PUG-REST patterns.
Convert between CID, SMILES, InChI, InChIKey, formula, and common structure file formats.
Fetch bioassay summaries, assay targets, active compound lists, and PUG-View annotations.
Use bundled Python helpers for compound search and bioactivity retrieval workflows.
limitations: Requires network access to PubChem or PubChemPy for live data retrieval.
Large similarity and substructure searches can be slow or rate limited by PubChem.
Downloaded structures may overwrite files when helper functions receive an existing filename.
The skill does not validate scientific conclusions or replace expert cheminformatics review.

use_cases.title: Compare Drug-Like Properties
use_cases.description: Retrieve molecular weight, LogP, TPSA, and hydrogen bond descriptors for candidate compounds.

use_cases.title: Build Compound Lookup Tables
use_cases.description: Convert compound names or SMILES strings into CIDs, formulas, InChIKeys, and canonical identifiers.

use_cases.title: Explore Bioactivity Evidence
use_cases.description: Find PubChem assay summaries, active compounds, and target annotations for a compound or protein.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Compound
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PubChem skill to look up aspirin. Return its CID, molecular formula, molecular weight, canonical SMILES, and common synonyms.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Properties
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare aspirin, ibuprofen, and naproxen with PubChem data. Include formula, molecular weight, XLogP, TPSA, donors, and acceptors.

prompt_templates.title: Search Similar Structures
prompt_templates.prompt: Use PubChemPy patterns from this skill to find compounds similar to gefitinib at 85 percent similarity. Limit results and summarize drug-like filters.

prompt_templates.title: Review Bioactivity
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bioactivity helper workflow to summarize PubChem assays for CID 2244. Separate active, inactive, inconclusive, and unspecified outcomes.

output_examples.output: Returns a concise compound profile with CID, formula, molecular weight, canonical SMILES, InChIKey, XLogP, TPSA, and selected synonyms.
Produces a readable comparison of key molecular properties and highlights missing values or likely drug-likeness rule violations.
Summarizes PubChem assay matches, active compound CIDs, source assay identifiers, and cautions about assay context and follow-up validation.

best_practices: Use CIDs for repeated queries because they are more precise and efficient than names.
Set MaxRecords and add delays for large searches to respect PubChem rate limits.
Check missing values before drawing chemical or biological conclusions from returned properties.
anti_patterns: Do not run unbounded similarity or substructure searches without result limits.
Do not overwrite local files with downloaded structures unless the path is intentional.
Do not treat PubChem assay outcomes as clinical efficacy evidence without expert review.

faq.question: Does this skill require internet access?
faq.answer: Yes. Live compound, structure, and assay queries use PubChem endpoints or the PubChemPy library.

faq.question: Can it search by SMILES strings?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for exact SMILES lookup, similarity search, and substructure search.

faq.question: Can it download structure files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can download SDF, JSON, and PNG structures when PubChemPy is installed.

faq.question: Does it include bioactivity workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. The bioactivity helper can retrieve assay summaries, targets, active compound lists, and PUG-View annotations.

faq.question: Is this a medical decision tool?
faq.answer: No. It retrieves scientific data, but users must validate interpretation with qualified domain expertise.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
